ticker.text: Leafs season ends with 3-1 loss to Ottawa
Draft Lottery May 5: Leafs hold 5th spot
Jays (7-10) at Brewers today, 1:40 PM ET
Cease dazzles but bats go cold in 2-1 loss
Playoffs: Raptors vs. Cavaliers, Game 1 Saturday
Raptors (46-36) back in playoffs for first time since 2022
NFL Draft one week away: Commanders pick 7th
Caleb Downs visit lights up draft buzz in DC

featured.headline: The Raptors Are Back. First Playoffs Since 2022, and Cleveland Awaits.
featured.dek: Toronto locked up the fifth seed with a 46-36 record and will face the fourth-seeded Cavaliers in a first-round series starting Saturday at 1 PM in Cleveland. The Raptors swept the season series 3-0, but those wins all came before the Cavs traded for James Harden in February.

two_up.headline: A Season of Dysfunction: How the Leafs Went From Division Champs to Last Place
two_up.dek: The Athletic details the organizational breakdown behind Toronto’s 30-point drop, from Marner’s departure to the AI-generated trade proposals that stunned the front office.

two_up.headline: Caleb Downs Visits Washington as Draft Buzz Heats Up at Pick No. 7
two_up.dek: Ohio State’s Jim Thorpe Award-winning safety visited the Commanders this week, with insiders pegging Washington as his “floor” landing spot. GM Adam Peters is weighing safety, receiver, and linebacker at seven.

extra_story.headline: Cease’s Gem Goes to Waste as Jays Fall 2-1 on Late Eighth-Inning Rally
extra_story.dek: Dylan Cease struck out six in six shutout innings but the Brewers rallied for two in the eighth. Toronto drops to 7-10 and plays the series finale Thursday.

today_slate.detail: Game 1 vs. Cavaliers: Sat Apr 18 at 1 PM

leafs.detail: Season Over · Last in Atlantic Division

lotl.label: What's Going On With the Leafs
lotl.body: The horror show is finally over. Toronto’s season ended Tuesday with a 3-1 loss to Ottawa, their seventh straight defeat, capping a 32-36-14 campaign that saw the franchise plummet from division champions to last in the Atlantic. The 30-point freefall from last year’s 108-point season ranks among the most dramatic collapses in NHL history, and the fallout has only just begun. The Athletic reported this week on deep organizational dysfunction: MLSE CEO Keith Pelley presenting AI-generated trade proposals to hockey staff, cost-cutting that hamstrung the front office, and a culture of friction between business leadership and the hockey department. GM Brad Treliving was fired March 30, and the search for a new head of hockey operations is complicated by the perception that ownership meddles. Auston Matthews’ season ended March 14 with a grade 3 MCL tear after just 60 games and 53 points. William Nylander finished as the lone bright spot with 26 goals and 73 points. The draft lottery sits May 5, where Toronto holds the fifth slot with a 24.5% chance of staying put. For a fanbase that expected playoff hockey, it’s ashes.

lotl.label: What's Going On With the Jays
lotl.body: Seventeen games in and the defending American League champions can’t find the offense that carried them to the World Series last October. Dylan Cease was brilliant Tuesday, striking out six in six shutout innings against Milwaukee, but the bats gave him exactly one run to work with before the Brewers scraped together a two-run eighth that sealed a 2-1 loss. It’s the story of the season so far: strong arms, silent lumber. Toronto sits 7-10, fourth in the AL East and three games back, with a .412 winning percentage that doesn’t match the talent on the roster. Vlad Guerrero Jr. has been steady but not spectacular, and the lineup around him keeps stranding runners in scoring position. The bright side? The Jays split the series with Milwaukee after Vlad’s go-ahead double keyed a 9-7 extras win Monday. The pitching is there. The bullpen held firm. What’s missing is the same thing that carried them last fall: timely hits and the confidence that comes with them. Patrick Corbin takes the ball Thursday at 1:40 PM for the series finale, then it’s home to face Tampa Bay this weekend.

the_latest.headline: Cease’s gem goes to waste as Brewers rally in the eighth to edge Jays 2-1
the_latest.dek: Dylan Cease was dominant through six shutout innings but the bullpen couldn’t hold a 1-0 lead. William Contreras drove in two in the eighth for Milwaukee.

the_latest.dek: Vladimir Guerrero Jr. ripped a double to right-center in the 10th inning, capping a wild back-and-forth game that featured 16 combined runs.

the_latest.headline: 2026 Blue Jays: Defending AL champs off to slow start at 7-10
the_latest.dek: Toronto brought back most of last year’s World Series roster but the offense has sputtered early. A look at what’s gone wrong and whether it’s time to worry.

lotl.label: What's Going On With the Raptors
lotl.body: For the first time since 2022, the Raptors are in the playoffs, and the vibe around Scotiabank Arena is electric. Toronto clinched the fifth seed with a 46-36 record, a 16-win improvement over last season, and they’ll open against the fourth-seeded Cleveland Cavaliers in a series that tips off Saturday at 1 PM in Cleveland. The regular-season numbers favor Toronto: they swept the Cavs 3-0 this year. But there’s a massive asterisk. All three of those wins came before Cleveland acquired James Harden in February, and the Harden-Mitchell duo has been lethal since, going 19-6 together. Scottie Barnes is the engine, Brandon Ingram earned an All-Star nod, and the wing-heavy lineup gives Toronto versatility that few teams can match. The concern is point guard Immanuel Quickley, who’s day-to-day with a hamstring issue heading into Game 1. If he plays, Toronto has the depth to steal home court. If he sits, the backcourt rotation thins in a hurry. Game 1 is Saturday at 1 PM on ESPN and Sportsnet. Game 2 follows Monday at 7 PM. The Raptors come home for Game 3 on April 23.

the_latest.headline: NBA Playoffs: What to expect in the Cavaliers-Raptors series
the_latest.dek: A deep dive into the matchup between the 4th-seeded Cavs and 5th-seeded Raptors, including the Harden factor and Toronto’s defensive versatility.

the_latest.dek: Toronto’s return to the postseason after four years is a story of patience, development, and the Brandon Ingram trade that changed everything.

lotl.label: What's Going On With the Commanders
lotl.body: The draft room hums with possibility in Ashburn, where a 5-12 season, nine games back, handed Washington the No. 7 pick and six selections to rebuild around Jayden Daniels. Ohio State safety Caleb Downs’ pre-draft visit this week lit up the wires, with insiders pegging the versatile ballhawk as Washington’s “floor” landing spot at seven. Downs won the Jim Thorpe Award and Big Ten Defensive Player of the Year, and his ability to play deep safety or slide into the slot fits Dan Quinn’s scheme perfectly. But GM Adam Peters is playing it flexible, eyeing impact over positional value: middle linebacker Sonny Styles, RB Jeremiyah Love from Notre Dame, or Downs himself are all in play. WR visits from Carnell Tate, Makai Lemon, and Omar Cooper Jr. scream receiver hunger opposite Terry McLaurin after Alec Pierce and Romeo Doubs left in free agency. Trade-down buzz swirls if elite tackles slide or edge rushers like David Bailey beckon later. Round 1 is April 23 at 8 PM from Pittsburgh. Watch if they pounce on Downs or wheel for volume.

the_latest.headline: 2026 NFL Draft: Latest Washington Commanders updates, intel, and buzz
the_latest.dek: ESPN’s insider roundup of where Washington stands at pick 7, including the Caleb Downs visit, receiver targets, and the trade-down scenarios in play.

the_latest.dek: Taking a safety at seven is rare, but Downs isn’t a normal safety. SI makes the case for why he’s the pick that transforms Dan Quinn’s secondary.
